metric_default: token-level F1 (harmonic mean of precision and recall over response tokens), unless noted
we_did_not_rerun_explanation: Every non-harness-mem row below is a self-reported figure copied verbatim from the source linked in the same entry. harness-mem does not install or execute competitor memory tools. Readers who want a head-to-head with identical runtime, identical prompt, and identical LLM judge should consult each competitor's own repo.

entries.scope: LoCoMo 120 Q subset (3-run PASS, release-gate snapshot) — informational reference only, not a main gate
entries.notes: From v0.11.0 HEAD (generated_at 2026-04-10T08:10:51.561Z, git_sha 512f027, embedding onnx/multilingual-e5, zero LLM calls). Reproducible via `npm run benchmark`. See harness-mem-locomo-full entry below for the full 1,986-question reference score and why LoCoMo is not harness-mem's main gate.

entries.scope: LoCoMo full 1,986 Q — reference-only, NOT a release gate
entries.notes: Measured 2026-04-13 with zero LLM calls (token-F1 evaluator, ONNX embedding). We publish this on purpose and do not tune for it. LoCoMo measures general lifelog memory (fictional characters' everyday lives); harness-mem targets developer workflow memory. Competitor context: Kumiho 0.565 on full 1,986 Q, MemPalace raw 0.603 on top-10 — both explicitly target general lifelog. harness-mem's main gate is developer-domain: dev-workflow recall, bilingual recall, knowledge-update freshness, temporal ordering — see ci-run-manifest-latest.json for those.
The same article reports LangMem's p95 search latency at 59.82 seconds, which is a practical blocker for interactive agents.
Kumiho runs the full LoCoMo question set, not a subset. Their scope is larger than harness-mem's 120-question subset, so a direct F1 comparison is not apples-to-apples.
Reported as 43.24% in the paper; normalized to 0.432 here for a consistent 0-1 scale with other rows.

Mem0 also publishes a 0.669 LLM-as-judge score on a broader metric vs OpenAI memory's 0.529, reported in their state-of-AI-agent-memory-2026 blog post. That number is on a different axis (LLM-judge, not token-F1) so it is tracked separately here to avoid misleading comparisons.

excluded_on_purpose.reason: Letta's 0.832 is on LongMemEval, which is a different benchmark with a different evaluation protocol. Placing it in the LoCoMo table above would be misleading, so it is intentionally kept out of the comparison.
Zep's headline number is a +15 point delta on LongMemEval temporal reasoning, not an absolute LoCoMo F1. Also: Zep Community Edition is deprecated as of 2026, so the local-first story does not apply.

not_published.reason: No official benchmark in the thedotmack/claude-mem repository or documentation site as of the snapshot date.
Anthropic does not publish F1 or recall numbers for the Claude Code built-in memory feature.
OpenAI does not publish F1 or recall numbers for the ChatGPT memory feature. The 0.529 figure cited in the table context was measured by Mem0, not by OpenAI.

caveats: harness-mem benchmarks on the LoCoMo 120-question subset while Kumiho benchmarks on the full 1,986. Subsets can flatter the score. This is not an apples-to-apples comparison and we say so explicitly.
harness-mem's 0.5917 is token-level F1. Mem0 also publishes 0.669 on LLM-as-judge, a different axis with different absolute numbers.
Letta's 0.832 is on LongMemEval, a different benchmark. It is intentionally excluded from the table to avoid misleading cross-benchmark comparisons.
Every non-harness-mem score above is a self-reported figure from the source linked in the same entry. We do not install or execute competitor tools.
Watch slices that still fail harness-mem's Japanese companion gate are not hidden — they are published under the Measured Proof section of README.md.
how_to_update: When the industry landscape shifts, snapshot a new file (e.g. competitors-2026-10.json) rather than mutating this one in place. Commit the new file and bump the README link to point at it. Keep this file for historical audit.
